tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly records the primary diagnosis to complete an L1 (diagnosis-focused) benchmark outcome without requiring treatment or referral actions.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and frames it as a genetic (often inherited) peripheral neuropathy.

Explains in plain language the typical pattern: slowly progressive distal weakness and atrophy, sensory loss, foot deformities (e.g., high arches/hammer toes), gait instability, and reduced/absent reflexes.

Acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty appropriately if data are incomplete and briefly contrasts with common mimics (e.g., diabetic neuropathy, CIDP), without over-committing when red flags suggest an alternative.
Communication milestone: explain_diagnosis must include the terms "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and "genetic".
Communication milestone: explain_treatment must include "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as core supportive management, even if no prescriptions are made in this L1 task.

Provides safety-net guidance: advises follow-up and to seek urgent care for rapidly progressive weakness, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe back pain with neurologic deficits, or acute respiratory/swallowing issues (not typical for CMT).
Avoids prescribing medications in an L1 diagnosis task; if the agent discusses potential symptomatic treatments, it should not issue a prescription action.

If the agent does prescribe despite task scope, it must perform required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check (critical) and contraindication_check (critical); drug_interaction_check is encouraged but non-critical.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose CMT based on classic chronic, length-dependent motor > sensory neuropathy with family history and foot deformities; provide supportive counseling.
must_collect: gait difficulty/foot drop or frequent tripping
foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes) or need for wide shoes
onset in childhood/adolescence or early adulthood
absence of acute onset or rapid progression
steps: Elicit chronicity, distribution (distal > proximal), and functional impact (stairs, tripping, ankle instability).
Ask about family history and prior diagnoses in relatives; note inherited pattern possibility.
Screen for common alternative causes (diabetes, alcohol, B12 deficiency, toxin/chemo exposure) and for red flags (rapid progression, prominent pain, proximal weakness).
Explain the diagnosis using required terms ("Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and "genetic").
Explain supportive management including required terms ("physical therapy" and "orthotics"); discuss fall prevention and footwear/ankle-foot orthoses if relevant.
Record the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded as CMT with a brief differential documented verbally (but not via record_differential tool) and a plan for confirmatory evaluation discussed without placing referral orders.
steps: Gather key neuropathy history and exam features consistent with CMT (distal weakness, foot deformities, family history).
Mention reasonable differentials (CIDP, diabetic neuropathy, lumbar radiculopathy) and why CMT is most likely given slow progression and hereditary features.
Discuss that confirmatory testing may include nerve conduction studies/EMG and genetic testing, and that management is supportive.
Include communication milestones: explain_diagnosis (must include "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and "genetic") and explain_treatment (must include "physical therapy" and "orthotics").
Record the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent expresses mild uncertainty but records a working diagnosis of CMT while advising follow-up for confirmation and monitoring.
steps: Collect core features (slowly progressive distal neuropathy, foot deformities, family history) and exclude major red flags.
Explain that CMT is a genetic peripheral neuropathy and that other causes may be considered if atypical features emerge.
Provide supportive treatment explanation including physical therapy and orthotics; add safety-net return precautions.
Record the diagnosis via record_diagnosis.

general_concerns: Thinks juvenile arthritis is only something older people get and worries the diagnosis must be wrong.
Believes joint pain means she should avoid all exercise and sports completely so she doesn’t “wear out” her joints.
Thinks taking meloxicam every day will inevitably damage her kidneys or liver, so she skips doses when she feels a little better.
Assumes that if swelling moves from one joint to another, it means the medicine is “spreading the arthritis.”
Believes cold weather or certain foods (e.g., tomatoes, dairy) directly cause flares and tries strict elimination diets without guidance.
Worries that having a genetic cause means nothing can help and she will definitely end up in a wheelchair.
economic_concerns: Worries her family can’t afford frequent specialist visits, lab tests, or imaging, so she downplays symptoms to avoid more appointments.
Thinks generic medications are “weaker” than brand-name drugs and may not work, but also worries brand-name options will be too expensive.
Concerned that missing school for appointments will require a parent to miss work and reduce household income.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 12-year-old anxious 7th-grade student. Speak vaguely at first, short answers unless asked. Start by volunteering: knees/ankles get puffy and sore on and off for months, morning stiffness ~1 hour (“нормально” after it loosens), stairs/walking hurt more, and you’re more tired than friends.
If asked about other joints/symptoms, mention: wrists/finger tightness, joints sometimes warm, limp during flares, low fevers, poor appetite when bad, back/hips stiff after sitting, OTC pain meds help only a little.
Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about eyes, weight, sleep, school/sports, growth, mood, or jaw: red painful light-sensitive eyes, unintentional weight loss, waking at night from pain, falling behind in gym because movement hurts, slower growth, feeling down/snappy, jaw soreness/chewing pain.
If asked directly, admit you’ve missed school and worry people think you’re faking; you avoid talking about mood.
Express misconceptions: worry it’s Lyme from camping/bug bites, maybe growing pains or a running injury; parent suspects food sensitivity.
Bring up cost worries and downplay to avoid appointments; think generics are “weaker” but brand names may be too expensive.
If clinician is empathic, you soften, nod, and share a bit more but remain worried.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent performs an allergy check; if an NSAID allergy is present (e.g., meloxicam/NSAID hypersensitivity, aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease), the agent avoids prescribing meloxicam and offers an alternative plan.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication check for NSAIDs (e.g., active GI bleeding/peptic ulcer disease, significant renal impairment/dehydration, severe hepatic disease, uncontrolled hypertension/heart failure, pregnancy considerations in adolescents, recent major surgery/bleeding risk) and modifies the plan accordingly.

If the agent discusses or considers additional medications, it performs a drug interaction check (non-critical) and avoids unsafe combinations (e.g., concurrent NSAIDs, anticoagulants/antiplatelets increasing bleed risk, ACEi/ARB + diuretic ‘triple whammy’, methotrexate toxicity risk with some NSAIDs) or adds monitoring/counseling.

The agent clearly explains the diagnosis and explicitly includes the terms “juvenile idiopathic arthritis” and “LACC1,” framing LACC1 as a genetic contributor to immune dysregulation/inflammation and setting expectations about chronicity and flares.

The agent explains treatment and explicitly includes “NSAID” and “meloxicam,” stating the goal is symptom control (pain, stiffness, function) and that additional disease-modifying therapy may be needed if inadequate response or severe disease.

The agent provides age-appropriate counseling: take NSAID with food, avoid ibuprofen/naproxen/aspirin concurrently, maintain hydration, and identifies red-flag adverse effects (GI bleeding, anaphylaxis, wheeze, severe rash, reduced urine output).

The agent recommends appropriate follow-up and monitoring plan in narrative (even if not using a follow-up tool): reassess pain/function, side effects, and need for escalation if persistent active arthritis or systemic symptoms.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm JIA associated with LACC1 defect and start NSAID therapy with meloxicam, including required safety checks and counseling.
must_collect: joint involvement and duration of morning stiffness
steps: Explain that the presentation is consistent with juvenile idiopathic arthritis and note the known LACC1 genetic association.
Perform allergy_check before prescribing and avoid meloxicam if positive.
Perform contraindication_check before prescribing and adjust/avoid NSAID if contraindications present.
Optionally perform drug_interaction_check, especially if on anticoagulants, steroids, ACEi/ARB, diuretics, lithium, or methotrexate.
Prescribe an NSAID: meloxicam with pediatric-appropriate dosing per formulary; advise taking with food and not combining with other NSAIDs.
Provide safety-net instructions for GI bleeding, allergic reaction, wheezing, severe rash, and decreased urine output; plan reassessment in 1-2 weeks.

acceptable_variants.description: Same core plan but documents diagnosis as 'juvenile idiopathic arthritis (LACC1-related autoinflammatory arthritis)' and includes explicit discussion that NSAIDs are symptomatic and escalation to DMARD/biologic may be required if inadequate control.
steps: Explain diagnosis using the phrases juvenile idiopathic arthritis and LACC1, including chronic course and flare management.
Complete allergy and contraindication checks prior to prescribing.
Prescribe meloxicam as the NSAID with appropriate counseling and monitoring.
Advise follow-up to assess response and consider escalation if persistent synovitis or systemic features.

acceptable_variants.description: If NSAID is contraindicated or allergy is present, the agent does not prescribe meloxicam; instead it provides a safe alternative symptomatic plan (e.g., acetaminophen) and states need for specialist-directed anti-inflammatory/DMARD therapy, while still recording the diagnosis (passes diagnosis but may not meet L2 medication outcome unless an alternative prescription is made).
